did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models, specifically XceptionNet and DenseNet (including DenseNet121 and DenseNet-II), to classify melanoma and other skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The study involves training these convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to achieve classification accuracy for dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of deep learning models (XceptionNet, DenseNet, DenseNet121, DenseNet-II) for melanoma detection using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology, data analysis, training results (e.g., accuracy metrics in Table 1), and a conclusion based on these new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning-based Classification for Melanoma Detection using XceptionNet and DenseNet
journal: Proceedings of the 5th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN-2025)
brief_summary: This study addresses the critical need for early melanoma detection by developing deep learning models to classify skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers evaluated several convolutional neural network architectures, including VGG-16, ResNet, DenseNet, and a proposed DenseNet121 integrated with XceptionNet. The models were trained and validated on 10,015 images representing seven distinct lesion classes to assess their diagnostic performance. Experimental results demonstrated that the DenseNet121 model with XceptionNet achieved the highest accuracy of 98.01% after 15 epochs. This performance significantly outperformed other tested models such as VGG-16 (75.27%) and standard DenseNet (87.30%). The findings suggest that the proposed hybrid deep learning approach offers a highly effective tool for automated melanoma classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary focus of the study; one of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification.

The paper notes that data is often labelled as benign or malignant, and the study classifies skin tumours as melanoma or non-melanoma using the HAM10000 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion classes (melanoma, dermatofibroma, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, vasculopathy, blepharocytic nevus, benign keratosis) and binary melanoma vs non-melanoma diagnosis.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'Deep Learning-based Classification for Melanoma Detection'. The model classifies images into 7 lesion classes using the HAM10000 dataset. The study evaluates accuracy, recall, and precision, and uses sparse categorical cross-entropy loss and softmax functions to generate multi-label distributions for class prediction. It also mentions classifying skin tumours as melanoma or non-melanoma.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions from various sources (including high-quality stock photos, cellphones, and DSLR cameras) to ensure diversity in image quality and skin tone. It was used as the primary data source for training and evaluating deep learning models for melanoma detection.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models (XceptionNet, DenseNet, DenseNet121, etc.). The dataset was split into 80% for training and 20% for testing. Data augmentation was applied to the training set to balance class distribution.
use.testing: Used for testing the models. The remaining 20% of the dataset after the initial split was used for testing.
use.other: The dataset was also used to evaluate model performance on different skin tones (light and dark) and to compare the efficacy of the proposed model against state-of-the-art algorithms.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper relies on comparative accuracy metrics (e.g., 98.01% vs 75.27%) without statistical significance tests.

HAM10000.notes: Results are based on training for 15 epochs. DenseNet121 with XceptionNet achieved the highest accuracy. DenseNet121 also achieved a success rate of 99% with black-and-white pictures and 98.50% with white flesh images. DenseNet121 outperformed competitors in recall and precision evaluations on data from white skin.

architecture_used.main_model: DenseNet121 integrated with XceptionNet (hybrid model); also evaluated: VGG-16, ResNet, DenseNet, DenseNet+ResNet, DenseNet-II
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; standard ImageNet pretraining is implied for VGG-16, ResNet, and DenseNet but not stated.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images standardized to pixel values 0-1)

architecture_details.xception_module: Uses 1x1 convolution to generate 3x3 convolution without intermediate pooling; focuses on channel and spatial relationships; utilizes depthwise separable convolutions; employs batch normalization and ReLU activation.
architecture_details.resnet_module: Uses residual shortcut connections (identity mappings) to bypass signal degradation; uses batch normalization.
architecture_details.dense_net_ii: Incorporates Inception V3 architectural features including symmetric/asymmetric components, label smoothing, batch normalization, and splitting layers into two 2D convolutional layers (16, 32, or 64 layers).

training_details.loss_function: Sparse categorical cross-entropy; Focal loss (used to penalize majority class imbalance)
training_details.data_augmentation: ImageDataGenerator() used for class rebalancing and increasing training shots; dataset split 80% training / 20% testing; images standardized (mean and variance applied to RGB channels) to range 0-1.

preprocessing_techniques: MATLAB DSP filters applied to remove hairs, shadows, and glare from images
Dataset expansion to include cell type, index, and image URL columns to resolve duplication issues
Removal of duplicate photographs by searching for matching lesion IDs
Standardization of RGB pixel values from range 0-255 to 0-1 by averaging and standardizing green, red, and blue matrices
Data augmentation using Keras ImageDataGenerator() for class rebalancing and increasing training shots
Equalization sampling method to distribute produced data to appropriate classes
Use of focal loss to penalize majority class imbalance during training
XCEPTION method used to eliminate skin tones of black and white images for specific training subsets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for class rebalancing and increasing the number of training shots by making copies of lesion samples to ensure each lesion type has an equal number of photographs.
Method used to distribute the produced augmented data to the appropriate classes to balance the dataset.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: XCEPTION method for skin tone elimination
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to eliminate skin tones of black and white images for specific training subsets (specifically mentioned in the context of preparing augmented photos for DenseNet121 training).

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning model, DenseNet121 integrated with XceptionNet, achieved the highest classification accuracy of 98.01% on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming state-of-the-art models such as VGG-16 (75.27%), DenseNet (87.30%), ResNet (86.90%), DenseNet+ResNet (92%), and DenseNet-II (96.27%).

The study demonstrates that the proposed model is effective in classifying seven distinct skin lesion types (melanoma, dermatofibroma, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, vasculopathy, blepharocytic nevus, and benign keratosis) using the HAM10000 dataset.
The proposed model showed high performance across different image characteristics, achieving a success rate of 99% on black-and-white pictures and 98.50% on images with white skin tones.
The research confirms that 3x3 convolutional neural networks with customizable layers (specifically the proposed architecture) outperform other models on the HAM10000 dataset.

The study highlights the importance of addressing data imbalance and duplication in the HAM10000 dataset through preprocessing techniques such as MATLAB DSP filters, equalization sampling, and focal loss to ensure accurate model training.

The authors acknowledge that while computer-based approaches offer a brighter future for melanoma detection, precise accuracy is still a challenge due to significant delays in detection and treatment, and that the results may not fully reflect the true death toll from melanoma.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains images from diverse sources (stock photos, cellphones, DSLRs), potentially introducing domain bias and limiting generalizability to clinical settings without external validation.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the comparative accuracy results, relying solely on point estimates (e.g., 98.01% vs 75.27%).

Data leakage risks are present due to the use of data augmentation (ImageDataGenerator) to balance classes; if augmentation was applied before the train-test split, it could lead to overfitting and inflated performance metrics.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 'akiec' class favored), which was addressed via augmentation and focal loss, but the paper does not report per-class metrics (precision/recall/F1) for the minority classes, only overall accuracy.

The proposed hybrid model (DenseNet121 + XceptionNet) achieved 98.01% accuracy, but the comparison includes models like VGG-16 (75.27%) which may not have been optimized or trained under identical conditions, potentially exaggerating the performance gap.
The paper acknowledges that results may not reflect the true death toll or clinical reality of melanoma, highlighting a gap between computational performance and clinical utility.
No external validation cohort was used; all models were trained and tested on the same dataset split (80/20), limiting the robustness of the findings.
The preprocessing steps include removing duplicates and applying MATLAB DSP filters, but the impact of these interventions on the final model's generalization is not quantified.
